title: The New York Fed DSGE Model Forecast—March 2026

source.name: New York Fed Liberty Street Economics

summary: The New York Fed's DSGE model revised 2026 GDP growth upward to 1.0% from 0.6% in December, driven by stronger investment (AI-related), while core PCE inflation projections rose to 2.4% from 1.9%, attributed partly to cost-push shocks possibly capturing tariff effects. The recession probability over the next four quarters fell slightly to 35.8% from 37.5%.
key_points: 2026 GDP growth forecast revised up to 1.0% (from 0.6% in December), driven by marginal efficiency of investment (MEI) shocks capturing AI-related investment strength
Core PCE inflation forecast revised up to 2.4% for 2026 (from 1.9%), with elevated readings also for 2027-2029 attributed to cost-push shocks possibly capturing tariff effects
Real natural rate of interest (r*) essentially unchanged at 1.9% for 2026; model now views current policy path as slightly more accommodative due to higher inflation
Recession probability (four-quarter output growth below -1.0% over next four quarters) decreased to 35.8% from 37.5%
Model forecasts produced before start of Iran war and do not incorporate its economic impact
DSGE forecast is explicitly noted as not an official New York Fed forecast, only an input to Research staff's forecasting process
why_it_matters: The model suggests the economy has surprised to the upside on growth while inflation has proven stickier than expected. The upward revision to near-term inflation alongside unchanged rate expectations means monetary policy may be somewhat more accommodative than previously assessed. This has implications for Fed rate path expectations and bond market positioning.
macro_context: The model attributes upside growth surprises to AI-related investment shocks (MEI shocks). Inflation persistence is linked to cost-push factors possibly reflecting tariffs. The model was produced before the Iran war began, meaning the forecasts are incomplete with respect to current geopolitical conditions.
notes: Source is a New York Fed research blog post presenting DSGE model forecasts. Topic is macro catalysts as it discusses economic growth drivers and inflation factors. Not market structure related. Published 12 days before TODAY (2026-04-01), well within recency window. Source is high quality from authoritative central bank source. Model limitations noted: not official forecast, pre-dates Iran war.
